pattern: Start from the score if every task used technique 2, then treat each task's technique-1-minus-technique-2 gap as an optional upgrade you would only ever take when it helps -- except you are forced to take enough of the least-bad upgrades to satisfy the minimum-count requirement.
hints: Imagine defaulting every task to technique 2 first, then decide, task by task, whether switching it to technique 1 changes the total by a positive or negative amount.
Sort those per-task swap amounts from most beneficial to least; you always want to take every positive one, since each strictly helps.

If taking only the positive swaps does not reach the required minimum count of technique-1 tasks, you are forced to also take the least damaging of the remaining (non-positive) swaps until you do -- so the answer is always the sum of the top max(k, count of positive swaps) swap amounts, added to the technique-2-only baseline.

edge_inputs.description: statement example 2, technique 1 dominates every task
statement example 3, k = 0 lets technique 2 win everywhere
single task, forced to use technique 1
k equal to n forces every task onto technique 1 even where it is worse
some gains are exactly zero, which should never force an unnecessary extra pick
more tasks favor technique 1 than the required minimum k

stress.description: large random task set with a random minimum count k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    t1 = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    t2 = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, n)
    return [t1, t2, k]
